topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the grain of his desk with a fingertip, the cheap veneer cool beneath his touch. The air in the open-plan office felt thick, stagnant, as if the oxygen had been deliberately rationed. He kept glancing at the framed motivational poster on the wall—a smiling silhouette against a sunrise—but the cheerful image seemed to mock the knot tightening in his stomach. He’d seen the preliminary documents tucked away in a seldom-used filing cabinet, a folder labeled simply "Restructuring." Every time he thought about opening it, a cold wash of dread flooded his chest, making his breath catch in his throat. He started tapping his pen against the notepad, a frantic, rhythmic sound that he hoped masked the sudden, sharp awareness that the foundations of everything he’d built here were about to shift beneath his feet.

[story 2]

“Just a routine audit, Mark. Nothing to worry about,” Sarah said, her voice unnaturally bright as she leaned over the partition. But Mark didn't look at her. His jaw was locked so tight he could feel the muscles strain beneath his skin. He kept running a hand over his closely cropped hair, a nervous habit he hadn't managed to break. The hum of the server room seemed amplified, a low, insistent drone that vibrated through the floor and into his boots. He could feel his palms growing clammy, slick against the smooth surface of his keyboard. The silence in his immediate vicinity felt deafening, every small sound—a distant phone ring, the squeak of an office chair—registered with alarming clarity. He just needed to finish this spreadsheet, just get through the next hour.

[story 3]

From his perch by the window, David watched the city sprawl below, the lights beginning to flicker on as dusk settled. He reread the email three times. The language was corporate jargon masking something far more sinister: 'strategic acquisition,' 'synergies,' 'future trajectory.' A tremor started low in his abdomen, spreading outward, making his hands shake slightly as he gripped the edge of the sill. He pulled his cardigan tighter around himself, a futile gesture against the sudden chill that had nothing to do with the evening air. He felt profoundly exposed, as if he were standing on a precipice, knowing that the ground beneath him was dissolving into uncertainty. He needed a drink, something strong, anything to steady the erratic pulse hammering against his ribs.

The air in the open-plan office felt thick, stagnant, as if the oxygen had been deliberately rationed. His jaw was locked so tight he could feel the muscles strain beneath his skin. He kept running a hand over his closely cropped hair, a nervous habit he hadn't managed to break. From his perch by the window, David watched the city sprawl below, the lights beginning to flicker on as dusk settled. He reread the email three times.
